title: Lesson 373
transcript: One more important type of discretionary access control that we need to talk about is attribute-based access control, sometimes referred to as context-based access control. So watch out for that on the exam. It can use those two names interchangeably. attribute-based access control or context-based access control. So what is attribute or context-based access control all about? Let's first remind you of something really important we talked about, which is zero trust. This idea of trust nothing, verify everything. This is a really good Security principle that a lot of organizations are adopting, which is never assume that you have any sort of trusted network. Never assume that whoever is trying to connect to your application is a trusted individual. Trust, nothing, verify, everything. And this is especially true as organizations move more and more things into the cloud. And one of the defining characteristics of cloud is broad network access. You can access many cloud applications from anywhere in the world from any type of device. And so we're tending to lose a lot of our layers of defense. And people that run network administrators now, organizations that are securing their networks should never assume that they have a secure network, that they have a trusted network. You should always assume that anyone that's trying to connect your application is a potential baddie. And so that's where attribute or context-based access control comes in. Because the idea here is rather than just, you know, like we have multi-factor authentication, but let's, how can we go even beyond that? You know, multi-factor authentication is excellent. You should absolutely be using it. But we can do even better than that. So the idea here is rather than just looking to see if they have the right username, the right password, the right one-time password, we can also look at a bunch of attributes related to someone's system and what they want to do and who they are to make a much more informed decision as to whether or not it's appropriate for them to access the application and even whether the specific action that they're trying to perform is appropriate. And so you can kind of keep doing this. You can keep watching what they're doing. And for each thing that they're wanting to do, make sure it seems appropriate by looking at all these different attributes. So what are some of the different attributes that we can look at here? There's a ton of them. But let's start to go through some of them here. So the first thing, kind of major thing you could look at is you could look at some attributes related to the user. So what's this person's role in the organization? How many years of experience do they have? What department, what country do they work in? What time zone do they work in? All this is really useful information to look at, really useful attributes. Take time, for example. If this is an employee that normally works Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Pacific Standard Time or something like that, and now they're suddenly trying to log in at 3 a.m. on a Sunday, that looks suspicious. Or, you know, let's say that they work in the... I don't know, Zimbabwe practice. And now they're trying to log into systems in Switzerland or something. That could look suspicious. Or let's say they work in the accounting department and they're trying to log into engineering systems. That could look suspicious. So by looking at all these different attributes, we can start to get a much more rich picture of like, is this appropriate? So we can look at a bunch of attributes related to the user. We can look at a bunch of attributes related to their system. We can look at things like their operating system. If every single employee in the company has been issued a Windows laptop and it's running Actually, let's start with this, the manufacturer. If every employee's been issued a Dell laptop and someone's trying to log in from a Lenovo or even more sketchy from a Mac, that would look suspicious. Or, got John to laugh at that one. Or if anyone's, if everyone in the employee, like all the employees systems, like operating systems, have been upgraded to Windows 11 and someone's trying to log in from Windows XP or a Linux distro, that would look suspicious. You can look at IP address, MAC address. You could look at the certificates that they have installed on their system. And a lot of devices nowadays even will have like GPS receivers in them. So you could even look at the specific latitude and longitude of someone logging in to see if that looks suspicious or not. So there's a ton of attributes we can look at related to their environment. There's a bunch of attributes we can look at related to the resource that they want to access. You know, do they want to access a database or a SharePoint site or an individual file? And what's the classification of the resource they're trying to access? There's a very big difference between accessing the company's internal wiki site, where there's just the company policies and other public information like that are posted, versus someone trying to log into... some super secure database or something, or even a very different type of web server. So the point is, you can look at a lot of the attributes related to the resource that they want to access, And one more major thing you can look at, you can look at what action do they want to perform. There's a huge difference in a database, for instance, between just looking up the value of a particular field and deleting the entire database, right? So you might be totally fine with someone just looking up a value, and you probably are not fine with someone dropping a database table. So the point is, attribute-based access control or context-based access control, you're looking at all these different attributes and you're feeding them all into what's referred to as an authorization engine. This is a piece of software that is essentially making a risk-based decision to determine, by looking at all these different attributes, the system is determining, is this appropriate or not? And this is often risk-based, and I'll give you an example of that in a second. Now, the way that this authorization engine is going to make a decision is based on a policy document that you have to create, which contains a whole bunch of sort of logic statements. So this is essentially how we program the authorization engine so that it can make decisions to ultimately permit or deny certain actions. So that ultimately is the idea behind attribute-based access control. And this is a brilliant idea. And a lot of organizations now are adopting this. And it really does help improve security. Now, one thing we have to dig into a little more detail here is related to this policy document, there's a specific language that you need to know about. It's known as XACML, the Extensible Access Control Markup Language. This is the language that's used to essentially write this policy document. So you can use XACML to, it's a sort of a markup language that allows you to architect and create these policy documents. So just remember XACML, the language that we use to write these policy documents. Now, I have a fun little bit of trivia for you here. Maybe when you're watching this video, you can think about this for a moment. Do you think you've ever encountered an attribute-based access control system or a context-based access control system? Do you think you've ever seen one of these yet? And I would bet a lot of you might be like, well, you know, I'm not sure. But let me ask you this. Have you ever seen this? Gone to a website and there's this button there that you're supposed to click that says, I'm not a robot. This is Google's new reCAPTCHA system. This, by the way, replaced this old horrendous system where we had to type in these mangled text to prove that we weren't a robot, right? And we all hated having to decipher this horrendously mangled text. So the new system just asks you to click a checkbox to prove that you're not a robot. If you've ever wondered, How does clicking a checkbox prove that you're not a robot? The way this works is it's an attribute-based access control system. Google knows an enormous amount about each of us. They know what devices we tend to log in from, what operating system, what times of day. They know a huge amount about us. And one of the other really cool things they track is your mouse movement. When you go to click on that little box, it's tracking your mouse movement. So, you know, how would a robot click on that box? Well, a robot would go like right to the dead center and click on it. How would a robot click on a box? Yeah, we'd like drunkenly stagger up there with our mouse barely catch the edge of the box, right? That's how we click on stuff, right? So the point is, Google is looking at all these different attributes to make a decision as to whether or not you're a robot. And the kind of cool part about this is you can actually tell it's a risk-based system. Because sometimes, you may have noticed this, sometimes when you click on the checkbox, it just lets you right in. Other times, it'll show you a grid of 3x3 pictures. What's happening there is that if the system's not entirely sure you're not a robot, it essentially makes you go through a second step of verification. And it asks you to pick out certain images in that 3x3 grid. Pick out all the stop signs, pick out all the buses, whatever. So this is an example of an attribute-based access control system. And this is one other important type of discretionary access control that you need to remember for the exam.
